name: Rue
description: Rue (Ruta graveolens), also known as herb-of-grace, holds a significant place in Mediterranean mythology and folklore. Revered for its potent properties, it was believed to possess the ability to ward off evil spirits, protect against witchcraft, and grant second sight. The strong, pungent aroma of rue was thought to repel not only malevolent entities but also diseases, making it a common addition to protective amulets and household remedies. In ancient Greece and Rome, rue was associated with healing and protection, often used in religious ceremonies and as a culinary herb. It was dedicated to Hecate, the goddess of magic, crossroads, and the underworld, further solidifying its connection to the mystical realm. Rue was also believed to enhance mental clarity and focus, aiding in divination and prophetic visions. Its bitter taste was seen as a symbol of repentance and purification, often used in rituals to cleanse the soul and banish negativity. The plant's association with sight and insight likely stems from its historical use in treating eye ailments. Across various Mediterranean cultures, rue served as a powerful symbol of protection, healing, and spiritual insight, deeply interwoven with daily life and religious practices. Its presence in gardens and homes was a constant reminder of the unseen forces at play and the power of nature to protect and heal.
subtitle: Herb of Grace, Protector Against Evil

keyMyths.title: Rue and Hecate
keyMyths.description: Rue was often offered to Hecate, the goddess of magic, as a symbol of protection and to invoke her blessings. It was believed to enhance the potency of spells and rituals performed in her honor.

keyMyths.title: Rue and Second Sight
keyMyths.description: Some believed that consuming rue or placing it under one's pillow could grant second sight or prophetic dreams. This association stems from its connection to vision and clarity.

sources.author: Dioscorides
sources.description: Ancient Greek physician's encyclopedia of herbal medicine, detailing rue's medicinal properties.
sources.text: Ancient Greek physician's encyclopedia of herbal medicine, detailing rue's medicinal properties.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Roman author's comprehensive encyclopedia, mentioning rue's uses and beliefs surrounding it.

extendedContent.title: Rue in Ancient Greece and Rome
extendedContent.content: In ancient Greece and Rome, rue was highly valued for both its medicinal and magical properties. The Greeks associated it with the goddess Hecate, queen of witchcraft, and used it in rituals to invoke her power. Roman physicians, including Pliny the Elder, prescribed rue for a wide range of ailments, from digestive issues to eye problems. It was also believed to enhance eyesight and mental clarity. Roman artists and intellectuals often consumed rue, believing it stimulated creativity and inspiration. Its association with sight extended to its use as an antidote to basilisk venom, a creature whose gaze could kill. The plant's presence was thus considered a potent safeguard against both physical and supernatural threats.

extendedContent.title: Rue and the Evil Eye
extendedContent.content: The belief in the evil eye, a malicious gaze that can inflict harm, was widespread throughout the Mediterranean, and rue was considered a powerful defense against it. Its strong, pungent scent was thought to disrupt the negative energy of the evil eye, preventing it from causing illness or misfortune. People would often wear sprigs of rue as amulets or hang them in their homes to ward off the evil eye's influence. In some traditions, rue was combined with other protective herbs, such as garlic and rosemary, to create even more potent charms. The plant's protective qualities extended beyond individuals, as it was also used to safeguard livestock and crops from the evil eye's malevolent effects. This belief cemented rue's place as a cornerstone of folk magic and preventative medicine.

extendedContent.title: Rue in Christian Symbolism
extendedContent.content: While rue had strong pagan associations, it also found a place within Christian symbolism. It became known as 'herb-of-grace' and was associated with repentance and forgiveness. The plant's bitter taste was interpreted as a reminder of the bitterness of sin, while its medicinal properties symbolized the healing power of divine grace. Rue was often used in religious ceremonies and rituals, particularly those related to purification and exorcism. Some believed that rue could protect against demonic influence and ward off evil spirits. Its presence in Christian contexts reflects the complex interplay between pagan traditions and Christian beliefs in the Mediterranean region. Rue served as a bridge between the old ways and the new faith, retaining its protective and purifying qualities while acquiring new layers of symbolic meaning.

habitat: Rue is native to the Balkan Peninsula but widely cultivated throughout the Mediterranean region and beyond. It thrives in well-drained soil and sunny locations.
shortDescription: Rue, or herb-of-grace, was a staple in Mediterranean magic, believed to ward off evil and grant second sight. Its strong aroma was thought to repel disease.
uses: Protection against witchcraft and evil spirits
Aiding in divination and second sight
